question: Which one of the following parts of human brain is the regulating centre for swallowing and vomiting?
explanation: The Medulla Oblongata has nerve centre to control many vital body processes like swallowing vomiting breathing and heart beat etc.

question: Which of the following statements are correct? 1. Normal consumption of calories among males is 2200 kcal per day. 2. Normal consumption of calories among females is 1500 kcal per day. 3. Post-meal, if the blood glucose level rises more than 160 mg then the glucose starts appearing in the body.
explanation: For men, the normal consumption of calories should be 2200 kcal per day, while in case of women, it should be 2000 kcal per day. Post meal, if the blood glucose level rises more than 160 mg then glucose starts appearing in the body.

question: What is the temperature of the body of a healthy person?
explanation: 98.4°F or 37°C is the temperature of the body of a healthy person.

question: Which part of the eye gives it a distinctive color?
explanation: • The Iris is that part of eye which gives distinctive color. • The retina is a layer of tissue in the back of your eye that senses light and sends images to your brain. • A small opening in the iris is known as a pupil. Its size is controlled by the help of iris. It controls the amount of light that enters the eye. • The front transparent part of the sclera is called cornea. Light enters the eye through the cornea.

question: Which of the following is a principal male sex hormone?
explanation: • Testosterone is the principal sex hormone in males and is produced in the testes (testicles). Testosterone is the sex hormone, C H O , secreted 19 28 2 mainly by the testes, but also in the ovaries and adrenal cortex, that stimulates the development of male sex organs, secondary sexual traits and sperm. (cid:1) The two main female sex hormones are estrogen and progesterone.

question: What will be the focal length when human eye lens becomes thin?
explanation: The change in the curvature of the eye lens can thus change its focal length. When the muscle are relaxed, the lens becomes thin. Thus, its focal length increases. This enable us to see distant objects clearly. When you are looking at objects closer to the eye, the ciliary muscles contract. This increases the curvature of the eye lens. The eye lens then becomes thicker so the focal length of the eye lens decreases.

question: The temperature of normal human body is
explanation: Normal body temperature varies depending on many factors, including a person's age, sex and activity levels. The Normal body temperature for an 98.60F (370C) adult is around but every person’s body temperature is slightly different and may consistently be little higher or lower. A Normal body temperature for children aged 3-10 ranges from 97.9–99.50F when taken orally. Children lend to have similar body temperatures to adults.

question: Glycolysis during fermentation results in net gain of :
explanation: In fermentation glucose (sugar) is degraded into ethanol or lactic acid producing only 2ATP molecules. One glucose molecules yields a net gain of 36 ATP molecules during aerobic respiration, which includes both glycolysis and TCA cycle.

question: Root hairs are produced from:
explanation: Cells of the epidermis produce root hairs near the root apex. These cells are generally thin walled, in contrast to the cells of the cortex, lying below the surface, which ultimately may become very thick-walled. 617 / 896

question: The storage form of glucose is:
explanation: When the body does not need to use the glucose for energy, it stores it in the liver and muscles. This stored form of glucose is made up of many connected glucose molecules and is called glycogen.

question: The onset of reproductive life is called-
explanation: The onset of reproductive age is called puberty. All living organisms must be able to pass on their genes to the next generation through reproduction. In humans, this is accomplished through sexual reproduction. The onset of reproductive age in humans begins in the early teens. • Maturation pertains to the process of becoming mature. • Menarche State – Menarche (say “MEN-ar-Kee”) is the occurrence of a first menstrual period in a female adolescent. • Menopause – When a woman permanently stops having menstrual periods, she has reached the stage of life called menopause. This stage signals the end of a woman’s ability to get pregnant.

question: The chemical substance present in bones and teeth is:
explanation: The chemical substance present in bones and teeth is Calcium Phosphate [Ca (PO ) ]. It is a 3 4 2 compound of calcium, phosphorus and oxygen. It is responsible for hardness of bones and teeth. The enamel of teeth contains 91 percent of calcium phosphate and hence it is the hardest part of the body.

question: The number of teeth which grow twice are-
explanation: The number of deciduous teeth in humans is 20. • Around 8 to 12 years all the deciduous teeth are replaced by permanent teeth. • The number of permanent teeth in humans is 32. • All the teeth originate from the same tooth socket in the jaw bone. • So, the 20 deciduous teeth that fall off are replaced by 20 permanent teeth. • The rest of the 12 permanent teeth grow once in the life time of a human. • This means only 20 teeth in man grow twice in life.

question: The hardest substance in the human body is :
explanation: Teeth is the hardest substance in the human body and most highly mineralized substance in body. It covers the outer layer of each tooth and it is the most Visible part of the tooth. The enamel is made up mostly of Minerals, Primarily by hydroxyapatite.

question: The adult human skeleton consists of-
explanation: The skeleton system protect the body and offers a structural framework. An adult human is composed of 206 bones. It is usually made of bones, cartilage, ligaments, and tendons that kind bones to muscles and muscles to bones.

question: The human body contains the maximum amount of:
explanation: Human body contains the maximum amount of water. The percentage of water in human body is 70% approximately. • Proteins are large, complex molecules. They to most of the work in cells and are required for the Structure, Function and Regulation of the body's tissues and organs. • Plasma is the largest part of your blood. It makes up more than half (about 55%) of its overall content. When separated from the rest of the blood, Plasma is a light yellow liquid. Plasma carries water, Salts and Enzymes.

question: Which is the emergency hormone in human body?
explanation: Adrenaline hormone is known as the emergency hormone. Adrenaline hormone is secreted during the time of stress or emergency by the adrenal medulla and is hence often referred to as the emergency hormone. Insulin is a peptide hormone secreted by the β cells of the pancreatic is lets of Langerhans maintains normal Glucose levels.

question: Which one of the following bones is not in the human body?
explanation: The pericardium, also called pericardial sac, is a double-walled sac containing the heart and the roots of the great vessels. • Humerus is the bone in upper arm. • Sternum or breastbone located in central part of the chest. • Tibia is the larger bone in lower leg. 618 / 896

question: Energy is stored in the liver and muscles in the form of :
explanation: Energy is stored in the liver and muscles in the form of Glycogen. Glycogen is a polysaccharide of glucose that serves as a form of energy storage in fungi and animals. • The polysaccharide structure of glucose shows the primary storage form of glucose in the body. • Glycogen is made and stored in the cells of the liver and muscles that are hydrated with the four parts of water. Muscle cells use fatty acids, glucose, and amino • acids as energy sources.

question: The normal temperature of human body is :
explanation: 98.6 0F – The average normal body temperature is generally accepted as 98.6 0F (370C). Some studies have shown that the normal body temperature can have a wide range, from 970F (360.10C) 990F(37.20C). to

question: The period at which organisms attains sexual maturity?
explanation: Puberty is the period of sexual maturity and achievement of fertility. The time when puberty begins varies greatly among individuals; however, puberty usually occurs in girls between the ages of 10-14 and between the ages of 12-16 in boys.

question: The total amount of energy released from one molecule of glucose on oxidation is about :
explanation: The glucose molecule is oxidised to form pyruvic acid, which is further oxidised to release water and carbon-dioxide. When one glucose molecule is completely oxidised, 38 ATP molecules are formed. The energy of the 38 ATP is equal to about 2900 kJ or 686 kcal of energy. The equation of the glucose oxidation is given as– C H O +6O →6CO +6H O+ 2870 kJ energy. 6 12 6 2 2 2

question: In a Human body, the size of the :

options.a: Right lung is larger than the Left lung
options.b: Right lung is smaller than the Left lung
options.c: Right lung is equal to the Left lung
options.d: Lungs changes & no fixed size Science

explanation: Lungs – The lungs are the centre of the respiratory system. This spongy, pinkish organ looks like two upside-down cones in your chest. The right lung is made up of three lobes. The left lung has only two lobes to make room for your heart. The size of right lung is larger than the left lung. Note:- The Alveoli are the structural and functional units of the respiratory system.

question: In the human body, the number of bones is :
explanation: The human skeleton is the internal framework of the body. It is composed of 270 to 300 bones at birth but this total decrease to 206 bones by adulthood after some bones have fushed together.

question: To prevent further injury in an injured casually, it should not be done:

options.a: Keep the casualty's injured part immobilized
options.d: Keep the casualty in comfortable position

explanation: To prevent further injury in an injured casually, it should not be done to move the injured part frequently.

question: What type of bandage is used in head injury?
explanation: Capeline bandage is used in head injury. If you suspect a skull fracture, do not apply direct pressure to the bleeding site and do not remove any debris.

question: Which of the following body part can be most affected due to ultraviolet radiations?
explanation: Exposure to UV (Ultra-violet) rays can cause premature aging of the skin and signs of sun damage such as wrinkles, leathery skin. UV rays can also cause eye problems. However skin is affected most due to UV radiation.

question: If a person vomits excessively and shows symptoms of diarrhea it is possible that in his blood serum there is a decrease of :
explanation: Sodium is an essential component for many of the body’s function which includes fluid balance, management of blood pressure and the nervous system. The condition called as 'Hyponatraemia' is caused due to low salt or sodium content in the blood. This also results from loss of sodium or fluid in the body due to severe vomiting or diarrhea. 619 / 896

question: Anticoagulant secreted by leech is :
explanation: Leeches produce an enzyme in their saliva called Hirudin. Hirudin is a powerful anticoagulant. They produce this to prevent their host from forming a clot so that they can feast on Blood more easily.

question: Which one of the following hormone is not synthesized by Islets of Langerhans?
explanation: Islets of Langerhans, also called islands of langerhans, irregularly shaped patches of endocrine tissue located within the pancreas of most vertebrates. They are named for the German physician Paul Langerhans, who first describe them in 1869. Normal Human pancreas contains about 1 million. The islets consist of four distinct cell types of which three (Alpha, beta, and delta cells) produce important hormones; the fourth component (C cells) has no known function. Beta cell produces insulin, alpha cell produce glucagon. The delta cells produce Somatostatin while Vasopressin is a peptide hormone.

question: The gap between two neurons is called a-
explanation: Neurons are the communication cells of the Brain and Nervous System. The Axon of one neuron and the dendrite of the next are separated by a tiny gap called a synapse. Synapse is also called neuronal junction, the site of transmission of electric nerve impulses between two nerve cells (neurons) or between a neuron and a gland or muscle cell (effector). • The typical synaptic cleft is about 0.02 micron wide.

question: Match the following and choose the correct option given below:

options.a: Adipose tissue → It is commonly known as
options.d: Connective tissue → Blood Connective tissue supports, prospects and given structure to other tissues and organs in the body. Science

question: Which organ on the body produces the fluid known as bile?
explanation: • Bile is a fluid that is made & release by the liver and stored in Gall bladder. • Bile helps in digestion.

question: Which is the largest gland of the human body?
explanation: Liver is the largest gland in the human body. The liver secretes bile, a digestive fluid; Metabolizes proteins, carbohydrates, and fats; store glycogen, vitamins, and other substances. Pancreas – The pancreas is a mixed gland. It is located in the abdomen. It is part of the digestive system and produces insulin and other important enzymes and hormones that help break down foods. Kidneys – The kidneys act as very efficient filters for ridding the body of waste and toxic substances and returning vitamins, amino acids, glucose, hormones and other vital substance into the bloodstream. • A gland secretes a specific substance in the body called hormone. • The Pituitary gland is the smallest gland of our body.

question: Which of the following hormones does NOT belong to the reproductive system?
explanation: The main reproductive hormones are estrogen and testosterone. Estrogen and progesterone are involved in maintaining the uterus lining • Adrenaline is a hormone released from the Adrenal Glands and its major action, to prepare the body for fight or flight. • Adrenaline hormone does not belong to the reproductive system.

question: Which of the following hormones is NOT a steroid?
explanation: Steroid hormones are synthesized by Endocrine glands such as the gonads, the adrenals and by fetoplacental unit, and are then released into the blood circulation. Vasopressin is also called antidiuretic hormone, is a hormone synthesized from the AVP gene as a peptide prohormone in neurons in the hypothalamus, and is converted to AVP.

question: In the context of hormones and their function in body, which of the following is NOT correctly paired?
explanation: The anterior pituitary gland also secretes Prolactin (PRL). During pregnancy, prolaction stimulates the breast development necessary for eventual lactation (milk secretion). Also, soon after delivery of a baby a woman's Prolactin stimulates the breasts to start secreting milk, a function suggested by Prolactin's other name, Lactogenic hormone.

question: In mammals, prolactin stimulates the production of-

options.c: Digestive enzymes in the small intestine

explanation: Prolactin is a hormone produced by the pituitary gland of mammals that acts with other hormones to initiate secretion of milk by the mammary glands. It also has other wide ranging function in the body from acting on reproductive system to influencing behavior and regulating the immune system. • Progesteron hormone is produced in the ovaries, the Placenta when a woman gets pregnant and the adrenal glands. It stimulates and regulates various functions.

question: Prothrombin is found in blood and is related with:
explanation: Prothrombin is a protein made by the liver. It occurs in blood plasma and it is an essential component of the blood clotting mechanism. Prothrombin is a plasma protein which is converted into thrombin in the presence of thromboplastin released by platelets at the site of injury. The thrombin converts protein fibrinogen into insoluble aid in blood clotting.

question: Male hormone secreted by the testes is:
explanation: Male sex hormones (as a group) are called androgens. Androgens are hormones that contribute to growth and reproduction in both men and women. • The principal androgen is testosterone, which is secreted by the testes. • A small amount is also produced by the adrenal cortex. • Testosterone secretion is regulated by a negative feedback system that involves releasing hormones from the hypothalamus and gonadotropins from the anterior pituitary.

question: A deficiency in the secretion of hormone from the thyroid gland leads to:

options.c: Delayed development of secondary sex characteristics

explanation: A deficiency in the secretion of hormone from the thyroid gland leads to hypothyroidism condition. In Hypothyroidism, there is a deficiency of both thyroxin and tri-iodothyronine hormone results in Science reduced metabolic rate, slow mental development, slow body growth, sluggishness (slow to respond), etc. The thyroid is an endocrine gland that secret three hormones that are thyroxin, tri-iodothyronine and Thyrocalcitonin.

question: Which of the following receives message from hypothalamus and communicates with other endocrine glands?
explanation: The body's Master gland or the Pituitary gland consists of anterior and posterior lobe, with each lobe secreting different hormones in response to signals from the hypothalamus.

question: Which one of the following enzymes is found in human saliva?
explanation: Ptyalin enzymes are found in Saliva. Ptyalin and Lysozyme both help in the pre-digestion of starches. It is also called salivary amylase. Ptyalin is starch splitting enzyme secreted by salivary gland, most active at PH 6.7. Pepsin and Rennin found in gastric juice, about 30% of starch digestion takes place in the mouth cavity. • Trypsin & Carboxypeptides enzyme help in the digestion of protein in the small intestine.

explanation: Matching column I with column II. Growth hormone Growth hormone → Anterior Pituitary Vasopressin → Posterior Pituitary Melatonin → Pineal gland Epinephrine → Adrenal medulla Cartioids → Adrenal Cortex 621 / 896

question: Which hormone is called as fight, flight and fright hormone?
explanation: Epinephrine (Adrenaline) is a hormone produced by the adrenal glands during high stress or exciting situations. This powerful hormone is part of the human body's acute stress response system, also called the fight or flight response.

question: The class of hormones that stimulates cell division in plants is :
explanation: Cytokinin (Ck) are a class of plant hormones that influence growth and the stimulation of cell division or cytokinesis in plant roots and shoots. Cytokinins are synthesized in the roots and are usually derived from adenine. They move upward in the xylem (woody tissue) and pass into the leaves and the fruits, where they are required for normal growth and cell differentiation.

question: Where in the human body are Meibomian glands found?
explanation: Meibomian glands are anatomically located in the tarsal plate of both upper and lower eyelids, as holocrine sebaceous glands that open directly on the eyelid margin and discharge their entire content onto the lid margin.

question: Which one of the glands in human body produces the growth hormone (Somatotropin)?
explanation: Pituitary Gland (Growth Hormone-GH) is present at the base of the brain and secretes a number of hormones. This growth hormone controls the development of bones and muscles. Thyroid Gland – The Thyroid Gland is attached to the windpipe and makes a hormone called thyroxin which contains iodine. The function of this hormone is to control the rate of Metabolism of Carbohydrates, Fats and Proteins in the body. Pancreas – Pancreas Gland is present just below the stomach and secretes a hormone called insulin. The function of insulin is to lower the blood sugar level.

question: The hormone, insulin is produced in:
explanation: Insulin is a hormone made by an organ located behind the stomach called the pancreas. Its function is to regulate the blood glucose level by allowing our body cells to absorb the glucose. Science

question: Which of the following is NOT a deficiency disease?
explanation: Gonorrhoea is a sexually transmitted bacterial infection that, infects both males and females. Gonorrhoea most often affects the urethra, rectum or throat. In females, Gonorrhoea can also infect the cervix.

question: The acid secreted by the gastric glands of human beings which helps in the digestion of food is ______.

question: Saliva converts :
explanation: Saliva contains the enzyme amylase also called ptyalin which is capable of breaking down starch into simpler sugars, such as maltose and dextrin, that can be further broken down in the small intestine.

question: Besides the protein-splitting enzyme pepsin, the fat splitting enzyme rennin, the gastric juice also contains a considerable amount of-
explanation: Besides the protein-splitting enzyme pepsin, the fat splitting enzyme rennin, the gastric juice also contains a considerable amount of hydrochloric acid.

question: Islets of Langerhans are present in :
explanation: Islets of Langerhans are islands of endocrine cells scattered throughout the pancreas.

question: Duodenum in human body is a part of :
explanation: The duodenum, first and shortest section of the small intestine, is a key organ in the digestive system. The duodenum has been described as a C- Shaped or horseshoe-Shaped. The duodenum measures approximately 20 to 25 centimeters in length. Science

question: Consider the following: 1. Amino acids 2. Fatty acids 3. Hormones 4. Vitamins Which of the above can be the sources of cellular energy in a human body?
explanation: Amino acids, Fatty acids and glucose are the sources of cellular energy in a human body.

question: Synthesis of prothrombin occurs in :
explanation: Prothrombin is a component of blood which helps in blood coagulation. It is mainly synthesize by the liver.

question: Length of small intestine of a man is approximately :
explanation: Although the small intestine is narrower than the large intestine, it is actually the longest section of the digestive tube measuring about 18 feet (6-7 meters long), on average, or three and a half time, the length of the your body. • Small intestine has three parts and have capability to from the layer of continuous bands of muscles around it. • Longer intestine provides larger surface area for absorption and assimilation.

question: Excess of amino acids are broken down to form urea in :
explanation: Deamination and urea formation is a major function of liver Deamination of amino acids is mainly done by liver (separation of amino group of amino acid and its conversion to ammonia). Preparation of urea from the amino acids is done by the liver. Liver converts ammonia (obtained from deamination) into urea through ornithine cycle. So after the damage or 623 / 896 spoilage of liver, the ammonia level in the animal body is increased and animal dies. Kidney removes wastes and extra fluid from human body. Spleen fights invading germs in the blood. Rectum is straight, 8 inch chamber that connects the colon to the anus. It stores faecal matters produced in the colon until the body is ready to eliminate the waste through the process of defecation.

question: Hydrolytic enzymes are present in :
explanation: Lysosomes are the small vesicle like organelles found solely in eukaryotic cells. They contain many hydrolytic enzymes such as proteases nucleases, and lipases. Golgi Aparatus enzymes-catalyze the removal of sugars from corgo proteins. Corgo proteins are modified by enzymes (called resident enzymes) located within each cisterna. Spherosomes are the membrane bounded organelle present in the cytoplasm of the cell. It helps in the storage and synthesis of fats. Microbodies are membrane bound vesicles that contain various enzymes, are present in both plant and animal cell.

question: Fat is digested in the :
explanation: The majority of fat digestion happens once it reaches the small intestine. This is also where the majority of nutrients are absorbed. Your pancreas produces enzymes that break down fats. Fat is completely digested in the small intestine because it gets bile from the liver through the gallbladder, which is responsible for fat digestion.

question: Name an acid which is secreted in the stomach?
explanation: The Hydrochloric acid in the gastric juice breaks down the food and the digestive enzymes split up the proteins. The acidic gastric juice also kills bacteria.

question: The liver and skeletal muscle in the body mainly stores ................ which is used as a source of energy.
explanation: The liver and skeletal muscle in the body mainly stores Glycogen which is used as a source of energy. Glucose is the main source of fuel of our cells, when the body does not need to use the glucose for energy it stores in the liver & muscles. This stored form of glucose is made of many connected glucose molecules and is called glycogen. Science

question: The number of Occipital condyles in man is –
explanation: Humans have two occipital condyles. They are large rounded kidney-shaped projections of the occipital bone, that are located contralateral to the foramen magnum and that articulates with the superiors facets of the atlas cervical vertebra.

question: During inhalation, the ribs .........
explanation: The process of taking in the air which is rich in oxygen is called inhalation. Similarly the process of giving out air rich in carbon dioxide is called exhalation. When we inhale, the ribs moves upward and outward while the diaphragm moves down ward.

question: During exhalation, the ribs .........
explanation: Exhalation is the act of exhaling air taken in and is passive. The diaphragm moves up and ribs move downward and inwards, decreasing the space in the chest cavity, this increases the air pressure inside the lungs and forces the air out of the lungs.

question: Normal range of breathing rate per minute in an average adult person at rest is____.
explanation: A person's respiratory rate is the number of breaths take per minute. The normal respiration rate for an adult at rest is 12 to 20 breaths per minute and a respiration rate under 12 or over 25 breaths per minute while resting is considered abnormal. • Breathing (or ventilation) is the process of moving air into and out of the lungs to facilitate gas exchange with the internal environment, mostly by bringing in oxygen and flushing out carbon dioxide.

question: During heavy exercise, breathing rate in an average adult person can increase upto per minute.
explanation: The number of respiration in one minute by a human is called breathing rate. During heavy exercise, breathing rate in an average adult person can increase upto 25 per minute. An adult person in an average respirates, i.e. inhale and exhale, 12-18 times in a minute in the state of rest.

question: Among the following respiration is which type of process?
explanation: Reactions in which energy is released called exothermic reaction. All combustion reactions are exothermic. Respiration is an exothermic process because in this CO presents in food breaks down to 2 form glucose and this glucose combines with oxygen in the cells of our body and releases high amount of energy. Photosynthesis and other microprocess are other example.

question: Match the items given in column (A) with those in column (B). Colume-A Colume-B I. Frog 1. Skin II. Leaves 2. Stomata III. Earthworm 3. Lungs and skin
explanation: The Frog has three respiratory surface on its body- the skin, the lungs and on the lining of the mouth. • The exchange of gases takes place in leaves Via Stomata through the process of diffusion. • Earthworms breathe through their skin because they do not have lungs.

question: Which of the following is present only in plant cells?
explanation: Plant cells have cell wall. It provides rigidity to the plant. The cell wall is a unique structure present in plant cells. It is not found in animal cells. • The cell wall surrounds the plasma membrane of plant cells and provides tensile strength and protection against mechanical and osmotic stress. Plant cell walls are primarily made of cellulose, which is the most abundant macromolecule on Earth. Cellulose fibres are long, linear polymers of hundreds of glucose molecules.

question: The net gain of energy from one molecule of glucose during aerobic respiration is :
explanation: There is net gain of 38 ATP molecules during aerobic respiration of one molecule of glucose. But in most eukaryotic cells, 2 molecules of ATP are required for transporting the NADH produced in glycolysis into the mitochondrion for further oxidation. • It is possible by the yield of ATP molecules from krebs cycle, glycolysis and electron transport system.

question: The breath that we throw out contains carbon dioxide up to an extent of :
explanation: The process of breathing and exhaling is called Respiration. Naturally, 0.03 percent carbon is present in the atmosphere, whereas the amount of carbon dioxide that a person releases while breathing is about 4 percent of carbon dioxide. Science

question: In which one of the following, is skin a respiratory organ?
explanation: The frog has a skin respiratory organ. A frog may also breath like a human, by taking air in through their nostrils and down into their lungs. Cockroach- Trachea, Shark- Gills, Whale- Lungs

question: Consider the following statements : Assertion (A) : When proteins are oxidized, the RQ value is less than one. Reason (R) : The proportion of oxygen to carbon in proteins is more, than in carbohydrates.

options.a: (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
options.b: (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not a correct explanation of (A)

explanation: The RQ (Respiratory Quotient) value is one during aerobic respiration. When respiration takes place in the presence of oxygen, it is known as aerobic respiration. It occurs in carbohydrates.

question: With increasing temperature, the respiratory rate will-
explanation: As the temperature increases, the kinetic energy also increases and hence increases the rate of reactions. Due to this the rate of cellular respiration also increases.

question: Urea cycle occurs in :
explanation: Urea cycle occurs in Liver. Urea Cycle converts excess Ammonia (NH ) into Urea in the 3 mitochondria of liver cells. The urea cycle or ornithine cycle converts excess ammonia into urea in the mitochondria of liver cell. The urea forms then enters the bland stream is filtrated by the kidney and its ultimately excreted in urine.

question: Which blood group is an universal recipient?
explanation: AB blood group is an universal recipient while O blood group is an universal donor. • Karl Landsteiner was discovered blood group.

question: Which of the following pairs are INCORRECTLY matched? Blood Group – Antigen – Antibody
explanation: Option (b) is incorrectly matched while rest of the options are correctly matched, because 'O' blood group has no antigens. Blood is made up of red blood cells, white blood cells and platelets in liquid called plasma. Our blood group is identified by antibodies and antigens in the blood. Antibody – Antibodies are proteins found in plasma. They are part of our body's natural defenses. They recognize foreign substances, such as germs, and alert your immune system, which destroys them. Antigen – Antigen are protein molecules found on the surface of red blood cells.

question: A person with blood group AB is sometimes called a universal recipient because of ............

options.c: Lack of both antigens and antibodies

explanation: People with AB+ blood group are universal recipients because they have no antibodies (AB) in their blood and can receive red blood cells from a donor of any blood type.

question: Lifespan of Red Blood Cells (RBCs) is____.
explanation: Lifespan of human RBC, in a normal individual with a mean RBC lifespan of approximately 120 days, this value may vary between 70 and 110 days. Human red blood cells are formed mainly in the bone marrow.

question: The lifespan of human RBC is about :
explanation: Erythrocytes are also known as red blood cells that deliver oxygen to and remove carbon dioxide from tissues. Erythrocytes have a limited life span and as they age, their membranes become more fragile leading to a loss of function and swelling. This damage occurs as the fragile cells pass through narrow capillaries, which occurs in the spleen. The life span of the erythrocyte varies from 70-160 days in domestic species. The average lifespan of RBCs in human is 120 days. Human red blood cells are formed mainly in the bone marrow and are believed to have an average life span of approximately 120 days.

question: Why one feel tired at the high mountain peaks?

options.a: Because of high pressure outside the body
options.d: Because of low pressure outside the body

explanation: We feel tired at the high mountain peaks because of low pressure outside the body, Altitude Sickness, the mildest form being Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS), is the negative health effect of high altitude, caused by rapid exposure to low amounts of oxygen at high elevation. Science

question: What is considered as the normal systolic/ diastolic blood pressure of a human being?
explanation: Blood pressure is considered normal if the systolic pressure in human is 120 mm Hg and diastolic pressure is 80 mm Hg where if the systolic pressure is more than 140 mm Hg and the diastolic pressure is more than 90 mm Hg then this stage is called high blood pressure or hypertension. Note : The pressure of the compressed state of the heart is called systolic and the pressure of the relaxed state is diastolic.

question: The blood vessels that carry the oxygen-poor blood back to the heart is known as:
explanation: The human blood circulation system has three main parts Heart, Artery and Vein. • Veins in the circulatory system are blood vessels that carry impure blood (Deoxygenated) from various part of body to heart. • Artery carries the blood purified by the heart to the various part of the body. The arteries are more muscular than the veins. 120 • Normal blood pressure of human mm Hg 80

question: –––– is a special fluid connective tissue consisting of a fluid matrix, plasma, and formed elements.
explanation: Blood is a fluid connective tissue that has a fluid matrix called Plasma and no fibers. • Erythrocytes (red blood cells) are involved in transport of oxygen and carbon dioxide.

question: Leucocytes are also known as––––as they are colorless due to the lack of hemoglobin.
explanation: White Blood Cells (WBC), also called leukocytes or leucocytes, are the cells of the immune system that are involved in protecting the body against both infectious disease and foreign invaders.

question: Which one of the following is considered as a rarest blood group?
explanation: The percentage distribution of AB negative blood group is below the 1%. So it can be considered as rarest blood group. Here is the average blood type distribution in the Indian population. Types Ratios O+ 38.4% O– 7.7% A+ 32.3% A– 6.5% B+ 9.4% B– 1.7% AB+ 3.2% AB– 0.7%

question: What are the main organs in the human body that regulate the pH of the blood?
explanation: • The lungs control our body by releasing CO 2 • The kidneys help to maintain the acid-base balance by excretion hydrogen ions into the urine & reabsorbing bicarbonate from the urine. • Heart pump enough blood to deliver a continuous supply of oxygen and other nutrients to the brain and other vital organs • Small intestine breaks down food from the stomach and absorbs much of the nutrients from the food.

question: With regard to human blood pressure, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The pressure of the blood inside the artery during ventricular systole (contraction) is called systolic pressure.
options.b: The pressure in the artery during the ventricular diastole (relaxation) is called diastolic pressure.
options.c: The blood pressure is much greater in arteries than in veins.
options.d: Arteries are the vessels that carry blood from various organs of the body to the heart.

explanation: Arteries are afferent (conducting or carrying towards the organs) blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart to all the organs in the body. The veins are afferent blood vessels that collect blood from the entire body and return blood to the heart.

question: Individuals with ............. blood group are called 'universal donors'.
explanation: Blood Group Donors Group A A, O B B, O AB AB, A, B, O O O (cid:2) It is evident that group 'O' blood can be donated to person with any other blood group and hence 'O' group individuals are called 'Universal Donors'. (cid:2) Persons with 'AB' group can accept blood from persons with AB as well as the other groups of blood. Therefore, such persons are called 'Universal Recipients'. Science

question: The Extra cellular fluid of blood is :
explanation: The body fluid that is non contained in cells is called extracellular fluid. It is found in the blood, in lymph, in body cavities etc. Plasma is the extracellular fluid of blood.

question: Plasma is a cloud of :
explanation: Plasma is a cloud of completely ionized matter. Plasma is considered the fourth state of matter. It is a cloud of protons, neutrons and electrons where all the electrons have come loose from their respective molecules and atoms, giving plasma the ability to act as a whole rather than as a bunch of atoms.

question: The blood pressure of a normal person should be :
explanation: Blood Pressure–Blood pressure is the force of your blood pushing against the walls of your arteries. Each time your heart beats, it pumps blood into the arteries. Your blood pressure is highest when your heart beats pumping the blood. This is called 'systolic pressure'. When your heart is at rest, between beats, your blood pressure falls. This is called 'diastolic pressure'. Your blood pressure reading uses there two numbers. Usually the systolic number comes before or above the diastolic number. For example 120/80 means systolic of 120 and a diastolic of 80. A normal person blood pressure is 120/80 mm.

question: Blood platelets help in :
explanation: Platelets are tiny blood cells that help your body form clots to stop bleeding. If one of our blood vessels gets damaged, it sends out signals to the platelets. The platelets then rush to the site of damage and form a plug to fix the damage. Red Blood Cell (RBC) production (erythropoiesis) takes place in the bone marrow under the control of the hormone erythropoietin (EPO). Juxtaglomerular cells in the kidney produce erythropoietin in response to decreased oxygen delivery (as in anemia and hypoxia) or increased levels of androgens. WBC is made in the bone marrow. They are stored in your blood and lymph tissues. Because some white blood cells called neutrophiles have a short life less than a day, your bone marrow is always making them. Monocytes are produced by the bone marrow from precursors called monoblasts, bipotent cells that differentiated from hematopoietic stem cells. 627 / 896

question: Which of the following carry the blood from all parts of body back to the heart?
explanation: The blood vessels which bring back the blood from various organs of the body to the heart are called veins. Through veins only impure blood flows, but through pulmonary veins only pure blood flows from lungs to hearts. The blood vessels which transport the blood from the heart to various organs of the body called arteries.

question: How many number of chambers are there in h u m a n h eart?
explanation: There are four chambers in the human heart which are divided by septum to each other. The two upper chambers are small and thin called Atrium. The two lower chambers are called ventricle which are also of two types right ventricle and left ventricle. There is a hole between every Atrium and their respective below ventricle which is called Atrio–ventricular valve. These valve open towards ventricle only.

question: What is the average life of Red Blood Cells in human body?
explanation: Red blood cells, also called erythrocytes are the most abundant cell type in the blood. The primary function of RBCs are to transport oxygen to body cells and deliver carbon dioxide to the lungs. The human Red blood cells are formed mainly in the bone marrow and are believed to have an average life span of approximately 120 days.

question: On an average how many million Red Cells are mm3 present in a healthy human adult man per of blood?
explanation: Red blood cells contains a substance called hemoglobin which transports oxygen around the body. The amount of oxygen that is delivered to your body's tissues will depend on the number of red blood cells (RBCs). The normal RBCs count is as following: million/mm3. (i) Male : 4.8 to 6.1 (ii) Female : 4.2 to 5.4 million/mm3.

question: Influenza disease is caused by which of the following?
explanation: Influenza (also known as the flu) is a contagious respiratory illness caused by flu viruses. It can cause mild to severe illness and at times can lead to death. The flu is different from cold. The flu comes on suddenly. People who have the flu have some or all these symptoms: Cough, Sore throat, Rumy or Stuffy nose, Muscle of body aches, Headaches, Fatigue, Feeling feverish/chills. Science

question: Which of the following element is found in haemoglobin?
explanation: Haemoglobin, abbreviated 'Hb' is an iron containing oxygen transport metalloprotein in the red blood cells of all vertebrates, as well as the tissue of some invertebrates. Haemoglobin in blood carries oxygen from the respiratory organs to the rest of body. There it releases the oxygen to permit aerobic respiration to provide energy to power the functions of organism in the process called metabolism.

question: The blood clot formed is due to presence of which of the following?
explanation: Blood clotting or coagulation, is an important process that prevents excessive bleeding when a blood vessel is injured. • Platelets & Proteins present in plasma works together to stop the bleeding by forming a clot over the injury.

question: Why is the colour of human blood red?
explanation: Red Blood Corpuscles (RBC) of human blood contains hemoglobin, in which hemoglobin is iron containing compound and due to this the colour of the blood is red. Globin is a proteinous compound and with hemoglobin it is extremely capable of combining oxygen and carbon-di-oxide forms an unstable compound called oxy hemoglobin and reaches to those cells by blood circulation where oxygen is needed. Normal range of hemoglobin content of an adult man is 17.5 gm/100ml of blood. Anemia disease is caused due to the deficiency of hemoglobin. Number of RBC is measured by an instrument called hemocytometer. In mm3 adult man it ranges between 4–5 million per of blood.

question: What is the protein inside red blood cells that carries oxygen called?
explanation: Hemoglobin is the oxygen carrying protein that is found within all RBCs. It picks up oxygen where it is an abundant and drops off oxygen where is needed around the body. • Hemoglobin is also the pigment that gives RBCs their red colour.

question: _____ are the factors responsible for blood coagulation.
explanation: The heme group (a component of the hemoglobin protein) is a metal complex, with iron as the central metal atom, that can bind or release molecular oxygen. Both the hemoglobin protein and the heme group undergo conformational changes upon oxygenation and deoxygenation.

question: Which of the following is referred to as a 'breathing molecule"?
explanation: Hemoglobin is referred as a 'breathing molecule" because the transport of oxygen in blood is undertaken by hemoglobin, the largest component of red blood cells. The protein collects oxygen in respiratory Organ's mainly in the lungs and releases it in houses in order to generate the energy necessary for cell survival.

question: Normal level of glucose per 100 ml of human blood is :
explanation: The expected values for normal fasting blood glucose concentration are between 70 mg/dL (3.9 mmol/L) and 100 mg/dL (5.6 mmol/L). When fasting blood glucose is between 100 to 125 mg/dL (5.6 to 6.9 mmol/L) changes in lifestyle and monitoring glycemia are recommended.

question: Electrocardiograph (ECG) is used to measure :
explanation: • Electrocardiogram (ECG) records the electrical signal from your heart to check for different heart conditions. It's common and painless test used to quickly detect heart problems and monitor your heart’s health. Science

question: Which one of the following elements is essential for the transmission of nerve impulses in nerve fibers of human body?
explanation: Sodium chloride and potassium ions are essential for the transmission of nerve impulses.

question: In the human body, red blood cells are produced in :
explanation: Red Blood Cells are also known as Erythrocytes. They are produced in spleen and liver (in the fetus) and in bone marrow in adults. The average life time of RBCs is 120 days.

question: Oxygenated blood is carried from the lungs to the –––––– by the pulmonary veins.
explanation: Pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the left atrium of the heart. All other veins in the body carry deoxygenated blood from the rest of the body back to the heart. Two pulmonary veins emerge from the lungs and drain into the left atrium. A normal human heart has two upper and two lower chambers. The upper chambers–the right and left atria – receive incoming blood. The lower chamber – the right and left ventricles – pump blood out of heart.

question: Average life span of human R.B.C. is :
explanation: The average life span of human R.B.C. is 120 days. Human Red Blood Cells (RBC) are formed mainly in the bone marrow and an average life span of approximately 120 days.

question: Father of Blood circulation :
explanation: 'William Harvey' is the Father of blood circulation. He was the English physician to recognize the full circulation of the blood in the human body. 'Karl Landsteiner' developed the modern system of classification of blood groups. Carolus Linnaeus is known as the Father of modern taxonomy. 'Paul Berg' is recognised for his contribution to basic research involving nucleic acids.

question: During the process of respiration of lungs, which of the following is pumped into blood :
explanation: The lungs and respiratory system allow oxygen in the air to be taken into the body, while also letting the body get rid of carbon dioxide in the air breathed out. Therefore during the process of respiration in lungs, oxygen is pumped into blood. 629 / 896

question: The lifespan of Red Blood Cells is ............. days.
explanation: Red blood cells are responsible for transporting oxygen from your lungs to your body's tissues. RBC's contain a protein called hemoglobin. It is formed in the red bone marrow of bones. The graveyard of RBC's is spleen. The life span of RBC's is around 120 days. Note-The life span of White Blood Corpuscles (WBC) is nearly 12 to 20 days.

question: Identify the wrong statement.

options.a: Diamond and Graphite are different forms of carbon.
options.b: Autopsy refers to the examination of dead body.
options.c: The brain is enclosed in a bony case called cranium.
options.d: The Red blood cells are produced at Spleen.

explanation: The following are correctly given : • Diamond and graphite are different forms of carbon. • Autopsy refers to the examination of dead body. • The brain is enclosed in a bony case called cranium. • The Red blood cells are formed in the bone marrow of bones. Spleen is known as the graveyard of RBCs. RBC are pulverized in the spleen where they are ingested by free macrophages. About 2.5 million of RBCs are destroyed in one second.

question: The chemical present in vehicular exhaust that is responsible for reducing the blood's ability to carry oxygen is
explanation: Carbon monoxide binds directly to hemoglobin forming carboxyhemoglobin and thus decreasing the oxygen – carrying capacity of the blood which in turn can result in adverse health effects on the cardiovascular system. Carbon monoxide emits from vehicular exhaust notably.

question: Red Blood cells are produced by the
explanation: Most Red Blood Cells, platelets, and most White Blood Cells form in the red bone marrow. Yellow bone marrow produces fat, cartilage and bone. White blood cells survive from a few hours to a few days, platelets for about 10 days and red blood cells for about 120 days.

question: Match the following veins/arteries with their transportations : A. Pulmonary 1. Delivery of veins oxygen poor blood from heart to lungs B. Pulmonary 2. Delivery of blood arteries to neck, head and brain Science C. Carotid 3. Delivery of blood arteries to kidney D. Renal arteries 4. Delivery of oxygenated blood from lungs to heart A B C D
explanation: Veins/Arteries Transportations a) Pulmonary Veins Delivery of oxygenated blood from lungs to heart. b) Pulmonary arteries Delivery of oxygen poor blood from heart to lungs. c) Carotid arteries Delivery of blood to neck, head and brain. d) Renal arteries Delivery of blood to Kidney Arteries–The arteries (red) carry oxygen and nutrients away from heart to body's tissues. Veins–The veins take oxygen pure blood back to the heart.

question: The pH of human blood is around :
explanation: Blood is normally slightly basic, with a normal pH range of about 7.35 to 7.45. Usually the body maintains the pH of blood close to 7.40. Note : Solution having a value of pH ranging 0 to 7 on pH scale are termed as acidic and for the value of pH ranging 7 to 14 on pH scale are known as basic solutions.

question: From which one of the following chambers of human heart is the oxygenated blood pumped into aorta ?
explanation: Left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood into the aorta via the tricuspid aortic valve. Functions:- The heart consists of four chamber in which blood flows. Blood enters the right atrium and passes through the right ventricle. The ventricle pumps the blood to the lungs where it become oxygenated. The oxygenated blood is brought back to the heart by the pulmonary veins which enter the left atrium. From the left atrium blood flows into the left ventricle. The left ventricle pumps the blood to the aorta which will distribute oxygenated blood to all parts of the body.

question: An artery differs from a vein in having :

options.c: Valves to control direction of flow of blood towards the heart

explanation: Arteries, veins and capillaries are the three main types of blood vessels.

question: Arteries: Arteries carry oxygenated blood from the heart to the different parts of the body (different organs). The walls of the arteries are 'thick' and 'elastic', due to high pressure of blood which is pumped out from the heart, and Elastic arteries so that it can contract and expend with each heartbeat. Two Arteries – Pulmonary artery – Umbilical artery Note :- Aorta is the largest artery in the body and it has to withstand very high blood pressure. 2. Veins: There is no pumping of blood so not need to with stand any pressure of blood thus it have not thick walls and elastic. They carry blood, that is low in oxygen from the body back to the heart for reoxygenation. 3. Capillaries: The capillaries are the smallest type of blood vessel and they are found in all blood tissues of the body. The walls of the capillaries are very thin and delicate so that they can allow nutrients and oxygen to diffuse from the blood into the tissues and vice-versa. 1358. Who discovered the blood group ABO for the first time?
explanation: Karl Landsteiner discovered the ABO blood group by mixing the red cells & serum of each of his staff.

question: Which of the following is not a type of white blood cell (WBC)?
explanation: Aldosterone is the main mineralocorticoid steroid hormone produced by the zona glomerulosa of the adrenal cortex in Adrenal gland. It is essential for sodium conservation in the kidney salivary glands, sweat glands, and colon. It plays a central role in the homeostatic regulation of blood pressure, plasma (Na+) (K+) sodium and potassium level. Erythropoietin:– It is a glycoprotein cytokine secreted mainly by Kidneys in response to cellular hypoxia, it stimulates red blood cell production in the bone marrow. Science

question: Consider the following statements : (i) Left atrium receives oxygenated blood from different parts of body while right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from lungs. (ii) Left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood to different body parts while right ventricle pumps deoxygenated blood to lungs. (iii) Left atrium receives deoxygenated blood different parts of body while left ventricle which sends it to different body parts. (iv) Right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from different parts of body while left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood to different parts of body. Which of the following statement/s is/are true?
explanation: The two atrium are thin-walled chambers that receive blood form the veins. The two ventricles are thick walled chamber that forcefully pump blood out of the heart. The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from systemic veins, the left atrium receives oxygenated blood from the pulmonary vein. Therefore (ii) & (iv) will be right.

question: What is the special nature of 'Collagen' in the human body?"

options.c: Moves the calcium in the blood stream

explanation: Collagen is the most abundant protein in body. It is the major component of cartilage, ligaments, tendons, bone and teeth. It is also responsible for skin strength and elasticity. It strengthen blood vessels and play a vital role in tissue development.

question: Coagulation of blood in vessels is prevented during normal circulation by :
explanation: Heparin is a natural anticoagulant of the body produced in the liver. It is normally found circulating through the bloodstream. It prevents the blood clotting in veins and arteries during normal circulation. Prothrombin is a protein made by liver. Plasminogen (PLG) It is the zymogen of plasmin, the major enzymes that degrades fibrin clots. In addition to its binding and activation on fibrin clots, PLG also specificially interacts with cell surfaces where it is more efficiently activated by PLG activators, compared with reaction in solution. Thromboplastin (TPL) is a mixture of both phospholipids and tissue factor found in plasma aiding blood coagulating through catalyzing the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin.

question: RH factor is a term that you will hear in connection with :
explanation: Rhesus (Rh) factor is a protein found on the surface of red blood cells. If blood lacks the protein called as Rh negative and if present called as Rh positive. Rh positive is the most common blood type. Having a Rh negative blood type is not an illness and usually, does not affect health. It should be tested before blood transfusion because it can harm the blood cell and other organs due to incompatibility of blood.

question: A person has 'O' type blood. Which test will be performed to confirm the blood group?
explanation: Agglutiongens in AB test will be performed to confirm 'O' type blood. Plasma has antibodies that attack both type A and type B blood. The type-A blood has a agglutinogen and anti-B agglutinins so it cannot accept type B or AB blood. Thus, agglutinogen AB test confirms the result that a person has O type blood.

question: The deficiency of which vitamin causes night blindness?
explanation: Types of Vitamins Deficiency Diseases Vitamin A (Retinol) Night Blindness B (Thiamine) Beri-Beri 1 B (Riboflavin) Retarded growth 2 B (Cyno Cobalamin) Anemia 12 C (Ascorbic Acid) Scurvy D (Calciferol) Rickets K (Phylloquinone) Excessive bleeding due to Injury Note-Deficiency of Vitamin diseases are diseases that are caused by the lack of certain essential nutrients, especially vitamins and minerals, in one's diet over a prolonged period of time.

question: The best source of protein among the following is :
explanation: Soybeans are high in protein and a decent source of both Carbohydrates and Fat (lipids). They are rich source of various vitamins, minerals and beneficial compounds. Protein– Proteins make up the building blocks of organs, muscles, skin and hormones. Our body needs protein to maintain our physique. Other sources of protein–Eggs, Almonds, Oats, Cheese, Milk.

question: Which is a water soluble Vitamin?
explanation: Vitamin–B complex and Vitamin–C are soluble in water while Vitamin – A, D, E, K are soluble in fat. It is important that Vitamin–C (Ascorbic acid) was first discovered. The deficiency of Vitamin–C causes scurvy disease. The main sources of Vitamin–C are citrus fruits like – lemon, orange, Indian gross berry etc.

question: Which of the following Vitamins is essential for blood clotting?
explanation: Vitamin – K is important for blood clotting and healthy bones and has other function in body. • Night blindness is caused by the deficiency of Vitamin–A, • Rickets is caused by a lack of Vitamin – D • Scurvy is caused due to lack of Vitamin – C • The term of vitamin is derived by "Casimir Funk" in 1912.

question: Anti-sterility vitamin is :
explanation: Vitamin E was first discovered by Evans and Bishop in 1922, and it was initially denoted as an “anti- sterility factor X” that was necessary for reproduction. Vitamin E-containing foods include wheat, rice bran, barley, oat, coconut, palm and annatto. Vitamin K is a group of vitamins that the body needs for blood clotting, helping wounds to heal.

question: _______ is mainly derived from citrus fruits and juices such as lemons, oranges and sweet limes/mousambis.
explanation: Ascorbic acid is also called vitamin C. Vitamin C is mainly found in citrus fruits and juices such as lemon, oranges and sweet limes/mousambis and vegetables. It is soluble in water and is an essential chemical element for the human body. It remains stable even in the air, but it oxidizes rapidly when mixed with a solution. The empirical formula of ascorbic acid is C H O . 6 8 6

question: Which food component has the highest gross calorific value?
explanation: Ethanol and fats have the highest amount of calorific value per gram i.e. 29 and 37 kilojoules per gram or 6.9 and 8.8 kcal/g respectively and proteins and most carbohydrates both have about 17kJ/g or 4kcal/g.

question: Amino acids are the building blocks of:
explanation: Amino acids are organic compounds that combine to form proteins. They are typically known as the building blocks of protein. Beyond building Proteins, they also assist in the synthesis of hormones and neurotrans-mitters. Made up of one or more linear chains of amino acids, in total there are 20 types of amino acids commonly found in proteins.

question: Beriberi occurs due to the deficiency of :
explanation: Beriberi is a disease caused by a vitamin B1 deficiency also known as thiamine deficiency. There are two types of the disease wet beriberi and dry beriberi. Wet beriberi affects the heart and circulatory system. Dry beriberi damages the nerves and can lead to decreased muscle strength and eventually muscle paralysis.

question: Scurvy is primarily caused by the prolonged deficiency of Vitamin ______.
explanation: Scurvy is a condition characterized by general weakness, anemia gingivitis (gum disease) and skin hemorrhages caused by a prolonged deficiency of vitamin C (ascorbic acid) in the diet.

question: Which of the following is primarily a source of Vitamin-C?
explanation: Vitamin C whose chemical name is ascorbic acid. Its main source is citrus fruits like Orange, Amla, Guava, Tomato, Chilli, Lemon and Sprouted Grains etc. • Its deficiency causes scurvy. • It's also vital for collagen synthesis, connective tissue, bones, teeth and small blood vessels. • The human body cannot produce or store vitamin C. Therefore, it's essential to consume it regularly in sufficient amount.

question: Deficiency of _____ in diet causes goiter :
explanation: Iodine deficiency is the most common cause of goiter. The body needs iodine to produce thyroid hormone.

question: Small amounts of iodine are necessary in our diet to____.

options.d: Compensate for underactive of the thyroid gland

explanation: Small amounts of iodine are necessary in our diet to compensate for under active the thyroid gland. When blood levels of TSH rise, the thyroid gland uses iodine to make thyroid hormones, However when your body in low iodine, it can not make enough of them. These hormones control the body’s metabolism and many other important functions.

question: Which vitamin is produced by sunlight?
explanation: Vitamin D is produced by sunlight. Our body prepares vitamin D in the presence of sunlight. The deficiency of vitamin D leads to rickets which makes the bones weak. It also leads to the decaying of teeth.

question: Which vitamin is required for formation of bones and teeth?
explanation: Vitamin D is required for formation of bones and teeth. Vitamin A – Needed for vision Vitamin B – Essential for producing various enzyme that help breakdown blood sugar. Vitamin C – Protect eyes and immune system. Vitamin K – Essential for blood clotting and helping regulate blood calcium.

question: Which vitamin among the following is produced in skin in sunlight?
explanation: Vitamin D is sometimes called the "Sunshine Vitamin" because it is produced in our skin in response to sunlight.

question: A deficiency of Vitamin-B can result in a 3 condition called :
explanation: Pellagra is a disease caused by low levels of Niacin also known as Vitamin B . explanation: Rickets in chickens is caused by Vitamin D 3 A list of major deficiency diseases that occur due to lack of essential minerals and vitamins. Type of Vitamins Deficiency Diseases A (Retinol) Night blindness B (Thiamine) Beri – Beri 1 B (Riboflavin) Retarded growth bad skin 2 B (Cobalamin) Anemia 12 C (Ascorbic Acid) Scurvy D (Calciferol) Rickets K (Phylloquinone) Excessive bleeding due to injury Type of Minerals • Calcium (Hypocalcemia) -Brittle bones • Phosphorus -Bad teeth and bones (Hypophosphatemia) • Iron -Anemia • Iodine -Goiter enlarged thyroid gland.

question: What is the cause of the Cushing disease?

options.b: Elevated levels of a hormone ( c) b a c t e ri a l i n f e c ti o n ( d ) F u n g a l i n f e c t io n

explanation: Cushing Disease:- Cushing syndrome occurs when your body is exposed to high levels of the hormone cortisol for a long time. • Cushing disease is caused by pituitary Adrenocorticotropic Hormone (ACTH) excess.

question: Which of the following is an excellent source of iodine?
explanation: Seaweed are the major source of iodine. The percentage of iodine in them is high as it develop in saltwater of the sea, about 4500 microorganism of iodine is obtained from a quarter seaweed.

question: Anemia is caused by deficiency of vitamins :
explanation: A lack of any one of several vitamins B , 12 folate (also known as folic acid), or vitamin C- causes vitamin deficiency anemia. Megaloblastic anemia is a type of vitamin deficiency anemia that specifically involves a lack of vitamin-B or folate. It caused body 12 tired and short of breath.

question: Pernicious Anemia is a disease caused due to the deficiency of :
explanation: Pernicious anemia is defined as a type of Vitamin B deficiency that results from impaired 12 uptake of Vitamin B due to the lack of a substance 12 known as intrinsic factor produced by the stomach lining. Vitamin B helps the body make healthy red 12 blood cells and helps keep nerve cells healthy. It is found in animal foods, including meat, fish, egg, milk and other dairy products.

question: Which of the followings is NOT a rich source of Vitamin B ? 12
explanation: Sunlight is not a rich source of Vitamin B .It 12 is commonly found in non-vegetarian foods. Meat, liver, kidney, oysters, fish, egg yolk are rich in vitamin B . 12 Vegetarians can obtain vitamin B from milk, milk 12 products, legumes and nodules of root vegetables (radish, beet, etc). question: The chemical name of Vitamin D3 is:
explanation: Vitamin 'D' (Cholecalciferol D3) is a fat soluble vitamin that helps your body absorb calcium and phosphorus vitamin D3, Chemical name – Cholecalciferol is only found in animal sourced food.

question: Which of the following amino acids cannot be synthesised in the human body?
explanation: The nine Amino Acids humans cannot synthesised are Valine, Phenylalanine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Methionine Leucine, Isoleucine, Lysine and Histidine. The six amino acid can be synthesised in human body are Glycine, Glutamine, Proline, Tyrosine, Cysteine and Arginine. Note-Amino Acid synthesis in the set of biochemical process (Metabolic pathway) by which amino acids are produced. The substrates for these processes are various compounds in the organism’s diet or growth media. For example human can only synthesis 11 out of the 20 standard amino acids.

question: Which among the following vitamin helps in controlling infection in human body?
explanation: Vitamin C is a water-soluble vitamin that has the ability to protect the cells of the body, including the immune cells from the harmful effects of free radicals. This increases life span of the immune cells boosts the ability of the immune system to fight disease. Vitamin C may improve the resistance of an individual. Good sources of Vitamin C are berries, citrus fruits, cantaloupes, green leafy vegetables.

question: Which vitamin is made at a higher rate in our body?

question: Scurvy is caused by deficiency of which vitamin?
